Q1: I tried to keep a structured routine, dedicating about 18 hours a week to studying, usually in the evenings after work. Being 28 and balancing multiple responsibilities, I found that writing things down and creating reading schedules helped me stay on track. I would block out time for reading course materials and taking notes, which fits my learning style. Some weeks were more chaotic than others, but I always tried to return to my baseline routine when things got disrupted.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I aimed for consistency, but deadlines would often force me into more intensive study sessions. When assignments were approaching, I'd shift my entire schedule around to accommodate them. The stress of juggling everything meant my 'regular' routine was more of an ideal than a reality most weeks.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements or seeing if there were discussion posts I needed to respond to. I logged in frequently—probably out of anxiety more than anything else—just to make sure I wasn't missing something important. Sometimes the sadness I've been dealing with made it hard to motivate myself, but the fear of falling behind pushed me to check in anyway. Notifications on my phone also prompted me to log in.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became almost automatic, like checking email in the morning. I set specific times to review materials, and it just became part of my daily rhythm. The high number of online courses I'm taking meant I was constantly in and out of different platforms, so the LMS became second nature. It helped that I genuinely wanted to engage with the content, even when I was feeling low.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most valuable to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I spent a lot of time with the course documents, highlighting and taking notes in my own words. The discussion forums were also helpful because they forced me to articulate my understanding, even though posting only six times doesn't seem like much. Videos were useful too—I watched quite a bit—but I always needed to supplement them with written notes to really absorb the information.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't complete as many assignments as I should have—only about 15% completion rate, which honestly embarrasses me. Some of the interactive activities felt less relevant to what I thought would be on exams, so I deprioritized them. When stress was high and my resilience was running low, I had to make hard choices about where to focus my limited energy. I also sometimes ignored supplementary materials that seemed redundant to what I'd already read.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to either the exam or my deeper understanding of the subject. I needed to feel like I was making progress, especially given how much effort I was putting in despite everything going on emotionally. If something felt like busywork or didn't have clear instructions, I struggled to justify spending time on it. The activities that let me write and process information in my own words always felt most valuable.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford, given my workload and mental state. Required activities had a sense of urgency that pushed me to complete them, even when I was struggling. With optional ones, I'd often think 'I'll get to that later' and then never did because something more pressing always came up. The guilt of skipping optional activities added to my stress, which created this frustrating cycle.
Q9: Absolutely. A lot of my studying happened with downloaded PDFs and my own handwritten notes spread across my desk. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd print materials or copy them into documents where I could annotate and reorganize information. I also used external resources—academic articles, textbooks from the library—to supplement what was provided. Much of my real learning happened offline, away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours reviewing my notes, rewriting concepts, and testing myself with flashcards I'd made—none of which showed up anywhere in the system. I'd download a video once and rewatch it multiple times offline while taking detailed notes. Sometimes I'd be deeply engaged with course concepts during my commute, thinking through problems, but obviously that's invisible to any tracking system. My assignment scores were high when I did submit, which reflected learning that happened entirely off-platform.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then spend days working through them offline at my own pace. This was especially true for longer readings and lecture slides—I'd save them to my tablet and annotate them during breaks at work or late at night. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the hours I spent with the material afterward. It was more efficient for me to work offline where I could focus without distractions.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult, usually when my stress peaked and the sadness felt overwhelming. Around midterms, I remember feeling paralyzed—I knew what I needed to do but couldn't bring myself to start. Work demands sometimes collided with course deadlines, and with my low resilience, recovering from those setbacks took longer than it should have. The mindfulness intervention helped somewhat, but there were still dark days where just logging in felt like climbing a mountain.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Fatigue was constant—being 28 with work and 14 online courses meant I was always running on empty. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because facing that confusion felt overwhelming. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage constantly, and the LMS often got the minimum viable attention rather than the deep engagement I wanted to give it. My high stress levels made everything feel harder than it probably was.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was completely elsewhere, consumed by worry or sadness. I'd watch videos and realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too anxious about other deadlines or life situations. Sometimes I'd log in and click through pages just to feel like I was doing something productive, even though I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt hollow, and I'd have to revisit everything later when I was in a better headspace.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate is so low despite my high scores when I did submit. The anxiety around starting something made avoidance feel like the easier option in the moment, even though it created more stress later. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts because formulating my thoughts felt exhausting when I was already depleted. I knew these things were important, but my emotional state often overrode my rational understanding of priorities.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense stress that sometimes felt unbearable, but they also forced me to act when I might otherwise have kept postponing. I'd often have bursts of LMS activity right before deadlines, cramming in reading and submissions. The structure of having fixed due dates helped me prioritize, but the pressure also contributed to my high stress levels and made the whole experience more anxiety-inducing than it needed to be.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though not always positively. When I received good feedback on my assignment work—which I generally did, given my high assignment scores—it motivated me to keep pushing. But waiting for feedback was stressful, and sometimes the delay made me disengage while I was in limbo. Constructive criticism was helpful but also triggered my anxiety about not being good enough, so I had mixed feelings about seeking it out.
Q18: The course structure provided a framework that helped me organize my efforts, which I needed given everything else I was managing. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me checkpoints to work toward. However, the structure also felt rigid at times, not accounting for the fact that life doesn't always cooperate with academic schedules. I appreciated the consistency but sometimes wished there was more flexibility for students dealing with high stress or personal challenges.
Q19: The availability of downloadable materials made engagement easier because it fit my read-write learning style and allowed offline study. The discussion forums were well-designed for peer interaction, which I valued even if I didn't post as often as I could have. What made engagement harder was when instructions were unclear or when there were too many small tasks that fragmented my attention. The sheer volume of content across my 14 courses sometimes made it hard to give any single course the depth of engagement it deserved.
Q20: I think that pattern could reflect someone who's genuinely trying to learn but struggling to translate that into completed work—maybe due to perfectionism, anxiety, or external pressures. They might be engaging deeply with materials but hitting walls when it comes to actually submitting assignments. It could also be someone using the LMS as a form of productive procrastination, staying busy without tackling the hardest tasks. I see some of myself in that pattern, honestly.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience. My exam score was decent and my assignment quality was high, even though my task completion rate was only 44%. I did a lot of learning offline that wouldn't show up in activity logs. Some students are efficient—they know how to extract what they need without excessive platform engagement. The LMS activity doesn't capture the quality of engagement, just the quantity.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing, and frequency of engagement with different materials. They can reveal when students are most active and how they navigate through content. But they miss the depth of cognitive engagement—whether someone is truly processing information or just clicking through. They also miss offline learning, emotional states, and the quality of understanding. A student could have low logs but high comprehension, or high logs but shallow learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just going through the motions. It's about connecting new concepts to what I already know and caring about understanding deeply. For me, engagement involves active processing—writing, questioning, discussing—rather than passive consumption. It also means showing up mentally and emotionally, which has been challenging given my stress and sadness. True engagement leaves me feeling like I've grown, even when it's difficult.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the emotional labor of pushing through when I'm feeling sad or overwhelmed. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices I've been using to manage my stress before study sessions. It can't see the extensive offline work I do, the conversations with classmates outside the platform, or the moments of genuine insight that happen away from the screen. Most importantly, it can't show that despite my struggles with completion rates, I'm genuinely committed to learning and growing—I'm climbing, even when it's hard.
